content:
{
  "title": "Program-of-Thought Prompting: Inducing Executable Pseudocode for Robust Mathematical Reasoning in LLMs",
  "problem": "Large language models (LLMs) often generate mathematically incorrect or logically inconsistent solutions to complex math problems, even with chain-of-thought prompting. Current prompting methods underutilize the structural rigor of explicit computation and program logic.",
  "motivation": "Improving LLMs' mathematical reasoning is critical for high-stakes applications in science and engineering, but existing approaches relying on natural language reasoning or step-by-step solutions remain error-prone. Bridging LLMs' language generation ability with explicit computation opens new frontiers for compositional and verifiable reasoning. Now, as LLMs increasingly support tool use and code generation, hybrid prompting that guides models towards executable logic is timely and promising.",
  "method": "We introduce Program-of-Thought Prompting (PoTP), a prompting framework that instructs the LLM to (a) translate math questions into detailed pseudocode or Pythonic code snippets capturing all computation and logic steps, and (b) explain each code step in natural language. Unlike standard chain-of-thought or sketching, PoTP enforces formal structure and variable tracking, enabling both stepwise verification and external execution. Prompts are constructed to request both the annotated code and the final computed result, explicitly discouraging speculative or 'hand-wavy' natural language inference.",
  "experiment_plan": "We will evaluate PoTP on GSM8K, MathQA, and MATH datasets. Baselines include standard chain-of-thought, self-consistency, and MathPrompter-like approaches. Evaluation metrics include solution accuracy, code executability, and error localization rates. Ablation studies will assess the impact of code-natural language interleaving versus pure code or pure text."
}
